exemplar.title: How does overloading 5-8% body weight with a weighted vest into a 5-week inclined sprint program affect 50-m sprint speed (ms-1) in 16-year olds with varying athleticisms compared to identical training without a weighted vest or no training at all?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title: How does overloading 5-8% body weight with a weighted vest into a 5-week inclined sprint program affect 50-m sprint speed (ms-1) in 16-year olds with varying athleticisms compared to identical training without a weighted vest or no training at all?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with a clear purpose and focus throughout the investigation.
Research question is clearly stated, narrowly focused, and continuously connected to every section of the essay.
Methodology is complete, replicable, and justified by current literature, with a clear visualization (flowchart) enhancing reproducibility.
weaknesses: Convenience sampling without a power analysis introduces selection bias and limits generalizability.
Definition of âmaximum speedâ is vague and self-gauged, reducing measurement consistency.
Baseline distance measurement involves multiple manual steps and unit conversions, increasing error.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently maintains a precise focus on the research question, communicates context clearly, and provides a largely robust, replicable methodology. Although minor methodological limitations (small sample, manual timing, vague operational definitions) prevent an absolutely flawless design, the work fully satisfies the 5â6 band descriptors for Focus and Method. A top-band score of 6 reflects the sustained clarity, purpose, and effective method selection that anchor the investigation.

strengths: Demonstrates excellent subject knowledge, integrating SEHS concepts (e.g. progressive overload, sprint kinematics, energy systems) with empirical findings.
Terminology and units (msâ»Â¹, %BW, ANOVA, Type I error) are used accurately and consistently, with properly formatted equations and statistical terms.
weaknesses: Occasional conflation of speed and velocity leads to conceptual imprecision.
ANOVA F-values are reported inconsistently, and the sample-standard deviation formula uses n rather than (nâ1).
High group variance (e.g. SD in control-group weight) is not discussed in the text, missing an opportunity to interpret group comparability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows strong, consistently relevant application of subject knowledge and precise use of discipline-specific terminology, meriting placement in the 5â6 band. Minor lapses in conceptual clarity and statistical reporting prevent a flawless performance; a score of 5 appropriately acknowledges the high-quality knowledge and understanding while reflecting room for improvement.

strengths: Multiple layers of quantitative analysis (descriptive statistics, paired t-tests, ANOVA, post-hoc tests) are conducted and clearly linked to the research question.
Discussion weaves results with theoretical frameworks and peer-reviewed literature, constructing a coherent, well-structured argument.
Evaluation identifies key methodological limitations and proposes practical improvements (e.g. photocell timing, quota sampling), demonstrating critical reflection.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation is somewhat compartmentalised rather than fully integrated into the narrative, with limited prioritization of the most impactful limitations.
Statistical reporting occasionally omits details (e.g. t-statistics, degrees of freedom) and uses imprecise interpretations of variability (e.g. SD magnitude).
Some sections of the discussion are verbose and slightly circular, detracting from overall conciseness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission exhibits good to very good critical thinking across analysis, argument development, and evaluation. Research analyses are relevant and well-linked to the question, arguments are coherent, and evaluations are reflective. While not exceptional in every detail, the depth and consistency of critical engagement justify a high-band score of 9 (within the 7â9 range) for Critical Thinking.

strengths: Adheres closely to IB Extended Essay structure conventions (title page, abstract, table of contents, numbered sections, bibliography, appendices).
Layout and formatting are consistently correct: margins, page numbers, captions, in-text citations, and properly typeset equations.
weaknesses: Some figures (e.g. Graph 1) lack axis labels and clear legend explanations.
Minor inconsistencies in image sizing and occasional font variance in code blocks.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets all the criteria for the top band (3â4) with a clearly appropriate structure and well-applied layout conventions. Minor graphical and formatting inconsistencies do not impede readability, supporting a maximum score of 4 for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The evaluation section identifies **multiple methodological weaknesses** (small n, manual timing, uncontrolled diet and attire, variable session attendance) and considers their potential impact on reliability and validity.  Practical improvementsâphotocell timing, indoor testing, quota samplingâare suggested, demonstrating reflective thinking.  However, the critique is somewhat compartmentalised rather than fully integrated into the overall argument, and the relative weight of each limitation is not always prioritised.  This constitutes a solid though not exceptional level of critical evaluation, placing the work at the lower end of the 7â9 band.

The research question is explicitly stated twice (title page and abstract) and is narrow in scope, specifying overload magnitude, intervention length, participant age and outcome measure.  Subsequent sections (methods, results, discussion) return to this precise wording when analysing data and drawing conclusions, demonstrating a continuous and logical link between the question and the unfolding argument.  The student avoids drifting into tangential issues and keeps all analysis anchored to the comparative effects of weighted-vest training versus unweighted or no training.  This level of focus aligns squarely with the top-band description of a "clearly stated, focused and effectively connected" research question.

Layout elementsâconsistent margins, page numbers, tables and figures with captions, in-text citations and a full bibliographyâare correctly applied.  Equations are typeset, and images are integrated with alt-text style descriptions.  Minor inconsistencies (occasional oversized images, slight variance in font for code blocks) are present but do not impede readability.  Overall, formatting meets EE requirements at a high level, satisfying the 3â4 band descriptors.

The essay follows the expected EE structure: title page with word count, abstract, clear table of contents, sequentially numbered sections, bibliography and appendices.  Headings and sub-headings mirror the table of contents, allowing easy navigation, and methodological, analytical and evaluative sections are logically ordered.  This adherence to IB conventions merits placement in the 3â4 band for structure appropriateness.

The topic is introduced through a precise title, a clearly sign-posted abstract and a concise statement of purpose that is reiterated in the introduction and throughout the paper.  The student constantly reminds the reader of the longitudinal nature of the investigation, the specific training modality (5â8 % body-mass weighted vest during a 5-week inclined-sprint programme) and the dependent variable (50 m average speed).  Key contextual informationâsuch as the scarcity of resisted sprint studies and the rationale for a 50 m dashâis provided up-front, giving the essay an unmistakable focus.  Because this clarity is maintained from the contents page to the concluding section, the topic is communicated "accurately and effectively with clear purpose and focus," fulfilling the 5â6 band descriptors.

Throughout the essay the student demonstrates **strong command of SEHS concepts** such as progressive overload, energy-system contribution, sprint kinematics and statistical inference.  Theoretical discussions (e.g. force-vector theory to explain sled vs. vest loading) are integrated with empirical findings and used to justify methodological decisions and interpret results.  Minor oversights (e.g. occasional conflation of speed and velocity, brief lapses in depth when discussing energy pathways) slightly reduce the sophistication, yet the application of knowledge remains consistently relevant and accurate, meriting placement in the 5â6 band.

Discipline-specific terminologyâground contact time, flight phase, ANOVA, Type I error, creatine-phosphate systemâis used accurately and woven naturally into the narrative.  Equations are properly formatted, units (m sâ»Â¹, %BW) are consistently stated, and statistical terms (p-value, F-critical) are employed correctly.  Apart from isolated typographical slips, the language shows a high degree of precision and consistency, fulfilling the 5â6 band for terminology and conceptual use.

The student conducts multiple layers of analysis: descriptive statistics, paired t-tests, one-way ANOVA and post-hoc tests, all reported with appropriate statistics (df, p, F, SD).  Findings are explicitly linked to the research question, and graphical representations aid interpretation.  Limitations such as sample size and measurement error are acknowledged, and comparisons with existing literature are used to contextualise the magnitude and direction of effects.  Although the depth of statistical treatment is commendable, some analytical choices (manual timing, modest discussion of effect sizes) prevent a seamless, "exceptional" analysis; nevertheless the work comfortably fits the 7â9 band for good, relevant analysis.

A complete, replicable methodology is supplied: participant selection, ethical consent, random allocation, detailed test and training protocols, progression rules, statistical tests (paired t-tests, ANOVA, post-hoc t-test) and sample calculations are all presented.  The student justifies design choices via current literature (e.g. rest intervals from Haugen et al., intensity targets, overload percentages) and identifies confounding variables.  However, some elementsâsuch as manual timing, very small sample size (n = 5 per group) and uncontrolled dietâreduce methodological rigour, and alternative designs are discussed only in the evaluation section.  These minor limitations prevent the work from reaching "complete and fully appropriate" status, but it solidly meets the 5â6 band for a largely appropriate and well-selected method.

The discussion synthesises quantitative results with theoretical expectations, weaving in a range of peer-reviewed studies to construct a coherent argument about the (in)effectiveness of weighted-vest overload.  Competing evidence is compared, and plausible biomechanical explanations (horizontal vs vertical force vectors) are proposed.  The narrative flows logically from results to implications, maintaining a clear line of reasoning.  Some sections are verbose and occasionally circular, but overall the argument remains focused and well structured, aligning with the 7â9 band for a well-developed, coherent argument.

annotations.comment: Although the 50 m dash is justified by acceleration phase literature, the rationale would be strengthened by referencing specific thresholds (e.g., mean split times) rather than general statements, adding depth to methodological evaluation.

Convenience sampling without power analysis introduces selection bias and limits generalizability. Acknowledging this under methodological justification would improve the rigor of source selection and design.
The definition of âmaximum speedâ is vague and self-gauged by participants; an operational protocol for determining treadmill speed should be provided to ensure consistency across sessions.
The interpretation of SD as âat least 85% magnitudeâ is imprecise; report coefficients of variation explicitly and relate to biological variability to strengthen analytical evaluation.
ANOVA F-value is inconsistently reported (7.51 here vs. 6.49 later); ensure calculations are consistent and show full computational steps to maintain reliability of statistical reporting.

Baseline distance measurement protocol involves multiple manual steps and unit conversions, introducing error. A more standardized method (e.g., a single calibrated tape) should be adopted to enhance reproducibility.
Figure 2 includes thorough alt-text and an embedded description, demonstrating excellent accessibility and presentation of visual data in line with IB guidelines.
The flowchart in Figure 5 effectively visualizes the progressive overload protocol, enhancing clarity of the method and supporting replicability of the five-week training design.

Justification cites meta-analysis recovery and intensity guidelines, but the deviation in session frequency is only qualitatively addressed. Quantify the impact of increased weekly volume to critically evaluate methodological choices.

The inclusion of a clear title, explicit research question and accurate word count on the title page meets IB formatting requirements, demonstrating attention to administrative detail and ensuring compliance with word-limit and titling conventions.
Table 2 leaves blank cells for pre/post group means in numeric columns, creating visual ambiguity. Ensure all cells display appropriate values or placeholders to maintain formatting consistency.
Graph 1 lacks axis labels within the figure itself and does not specify error-bar meanings clearly; include axis titles and legend details in the graphic to improve readability.
The post-hoc t-test between WV and EG concludes p=0.23 without reporting t-statistics or degrees of freedom; include full test details to support critical evaluation of comparative findings.

The discussion of sound-delay (speed of sound) as a factor in manual timing is misplaced; timing error relates to human reaction time, not acoustic propagation, indicating a misunderstanding of measurement error sources.

Bibliography entries vary in formatting (italics, DOI placement, list style). Standardize citation style (e.g., consistent use of italics for journal titles and inclusion of DOIs) to meet presentation criteria.

The abstract accurately applies subject-specific terminology (e.g., âmean Â± SDâ, âmsâ»Â¹â) and concisely summarizes objectives, methods and results, demonstrating strong knowledge and understanding.
Typographical error in âunpaiaredâ t-test and inconsistent sentence structure in methods sections should be corrected for clarity and professionalism in presentation.
A comprehensive table of contents with accurate page references and consistent formatting enhances navigation and demonstrates adherence to expected extendedâessay structure conventions.

The research question is stated precisely in the title section, specifying variables, participant age and comparison groups, which anchors the investigation with clear focus and purpose throughout the essay.
The abstract maintains a clear connection to the research question when summarizing methods and findings, reinforcing focus and ensuring coherence between the question and the reported outcomes.

The definition conflates speed and velocity (ârate of change of positionâ vs vector quantity). Clarify that speed is scalar magnitude to avoid conceptual confusion and ensure precise use of terminology.

Parenthetical citations vary in style (e.g., âCronin, 2005â vs â(Mero, et al. 2012)â). Standardize citation formatting (authorâdate) consistently throughout the text to meet EE presentation conventions.

The literature review establishes the research gap by identifying that progressive overloading of weighted vests is sporadically tested, effectively justifying the investigationâs originality and maintaining clear focus.
The research question and hypotheses are restated clearly and connected sequentially to the methods, creating a logical thread that upholds focus and coherence across sections.
Table 1 presents group anthropometrics with a notably high SD for CG weight (19.4 kg) that is not discussed in the text; interpreting this variance would deepen insights into group comparability.
Units and terminology in the results table (âmsâ»Â¹â, âÎ%â, âSDâ) are consistently and accurately applied, reflecting strong subject knowledge and precision.

The evaluation section identifies multiple limitations (sample size, athleticism variance, measurement error) and proposes methodological improvements, demonstrating a consistent critical evaluation of the study.
Sample-standard deviation formula should use denominator (nâ1) rather than n to align with inferential statistics conventions; correct this to ensure accurate SD estimation.

The participant handout clearly outlines the training protocol, but would benefit from specifying safety procedures and criteria for terminations within the main methods rather than appendix for direct methodological transparency.
